raw_case:
Isolated testicular tuberculosis: A case report
Genitourinary tuberculosis (GUTB) constitutes 20 % of extrapulmonary TB cases, with isolated testicular involvement being rare (2–4%). This case details a 41-year-old male with chronic left scrotal swelling, fistulization, and purulent discharge, ultimately diagnosed with testicular tuberculosis. Diagnostic imaging and histopathology confirmed caseating granulomas and acid-fast bacilli. The patient underwent high inguinal orchidectomy and was treated with a standard four-drug anti-tuberculosis regimen. Postoperative semen analysis revealed persistent azoospermia, indicating permanent fertility impairment. This case underscores the importance of considering GUTB in differential diagnoses of testicular masses, particularly in TB-endemic regions.

Highlights

•Rare case of testicular tuberculosis in a 41-year-old with chronic scrotal swelling and purulent discharge.•Diagnosis confirmed via histopathology showing caseating granulomas and acid-fast bacilli.•Persistent azoospermia post-treatment highlights potential for permanent fertility impairment.1

Introduction

Genitourinary tuberculosis (GUTB) constitutes 20 % of extrapulmonary TB cases, with kidneys most commonly affected.1 Isolated testicular involvement is rare, occurring in only 2–4% of GUTB cases. Tuberculous orchitis can resemble testicular tumors, complicating diagnosis, especially in high TB prevalence regions.2 In Morocco, TB is endemic, with an incidence of 97 cases per 100,000 people annually. This case highlights the need for increased awareness and diagnostic vigilance.2

Case presentation

A 41-year-old male, with no significant medical history, presented with a chronic left scrotal swelling persisting for over eight weeks. The mass had progressively increased in size, reaching a diameter of 15 cm, and had fistulized three weeks prior to presentation, discharging purulent material. The patient reported no systemic symptoms such as fever, night sweats, weight loss, or signs suggestive of pulmonary tuberculosis. A chest X-ray was performed to rule out any pulmonary involvement and returned normal, eliminating the possibility of active pulmonary tuberculosis. He also denied any urinary symptoms, prior scrotal trauma, or history of tuberculosis exposure. Physical examination revealed a tense, enlarged left hemiscrotum with a fistulous opening at the lower pole discharging thick purulent fluid. The left testis was firm, irregular, and non-tender, while the right testis was normal. There was no palpable lymphadenopathy, and systemic examination, including the chest and abdomen, was unremarkable (Fig. 1).A scrotal ultrasound showed an enlarged left testis measuring 48 mm × 36 mm x 33 mm with a heterogeneous echotexture and multiple hypoechoic, necrotic hemorrhagic areas, non-vascularized on Doppler. Irregular microcalcifications were scattered throughout the testicular tissue. The scrotal wall appeared thickened (9 mm) with infiltration of the surrounding tissues. The epididymis was also enlarged (17 mm × 12 mm) with a heterogeneous structure, raising suspicion for a neoplastic process. Tumor markers, including alpha-fetoprotein (AFP), beta-hCG, and LDH, were all within normal limits. A complete blood count revealed mild anemia (Hb 13.2 g/dL) with a slight elevation in the erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) at 42 mm/h. A QuantiFERON-TB Gold test returned positive.A contrast-enhanced CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis was performed to rule out any associated renal or bladder tuberculosis, and no other genitourinary or systemic lesions were identified. Given the concern for malignancy, a left high inguinal orchidectomy was performed. Perioperative findings revealed a grossly enlarged testis with a necrotic, heterogeneous appearance. The testicular parenchyma was largely replaced by caseous, yellowish material, and the surrounding tunica was thickened and infiltrated, consistent with an infectious process (Fig. 2).Histopathological examination of the excised testis revealed caseating granulomas with central necrosis, characteristic of tuberculosis. Acid-fast bacilli were identified on Ziehl-Neelsen staining, confirming the diagnosis. There was no evidence of malignancy (Fig. 3).Following surgery, the patient was started on a standard four-drug anti-tuberculosis regimen, consisting of: Isoniazid (H) 300 mg/day, Rifampin (R) 600 mg/day, Pyrazinamide (Z) 1500 mg/day, and Ethambutol (E) 1200 mg/day. This intensive phase was planned for two months, followed by a continuation phase with isoniazid and rifampin for an additional four months. The patient's liver function tests were monitored monthly due to the hepatotoxic potential of these medications, especially with the use of isoniazid and rifampin. Visual acuity and color vision testing were conducted regularly to monitor for ethambutol-induced optic neuropathy. The patient tolerated the treatment well, and no significant side effects were reported.Regarding fertility, the patient's semen analysis was performed preoperatively and postoperatively. The initial analysis revealed azoospermia, likely secondary to the advanced necrotic involvement of the testicle and epididymis. A follow-up semen analysis three months post-orchidectomy confirmed persistent azoospermia, suggesting permanent impairment of fertility. Given the unilateral nature of the disease, the remaining testis was monitored for any signs of compensatory hypertrophy or functional recovery. Testosterone levels remained within normal limits, and the patient reported no symptoms of hypogonadism.3

Discussion

Genitourinary tuberculosis (GUTB) accounts for approximately 27 % of all cases of extrapulmonary tuberculosis, making it the second most common form after lymphatic involvement.1 GUTB predominantly affects the kidneys, followed by the ureters and bladder, while the external genital organs are less frequently involved. Isolated testicular tuberculosis is a rare presentation, constituting only 2–3% of GUTB cases, with most cases involving concomitant epididymal or prostatic disease.2The pathogenesis of testicular tuberculosis typically involves hematogenous spread from a primary pulmonary or extrapulmonary focus.3 This dissemination is seen in up to 50 % of GUTB cases, with concurrent pulmonary TB detected in 20–30 % of patients. However, in regions where tuberculosis is endemic, up to 10 % of patients with GUTB may present without any evident primary focus, making isolated genital TB difficult to diagnose.1The clinical presentation of testicular tuberculosis is often nonspecific, mimicking conditions such as bacterial orchitis, abscesses, or malignancy. Common symptoms include painless scrotal swelling (observed in 60–70 % of cases), pain (30–40 %), and, in advanced cases, fistula formation. Fistulas are more common in patients with delayed diagnosis or inadequate treatment, occurring in approximately 10–15 % of advanced cases.2Ultrasound is the primary imaging modality, with sensitivity rates for detecting testicular TB exceeding 80 %.3 Characteristic findings include a heterogeneous testicular echotexture with hypoechoic necrotic areas and calcifications, present in 50–60 % of cases. Doppler ultrasound may show an absence of vascular flow in necrotic areas, distinguishing it from malignancy. Scrotal wall thickening and epididymal involvement, seen in about 60–70 % of testicular TB cases, are also key diagnostic indicators.3Histopathology remains the gold standard for diagnosing testicular tuberculosis. Typical findings include caseating granulomas with central necrosis, and acid-fast bacilli can be detected in 30–50 % of cases through Ziehl-Neelsen staining. Granulomatous inflammation with caseation is highly specific for TB.4 In contrast, non-caseating granulomas may be seen in other conditions such as sarcoidosis or idiopathic granulomatous orchitis, making TB difficult to exclude without microbiological confirmation.1The diagnosis of GUTB may be further supported by microbiological testing, with urine cultures yielding Mycobacterium tuberculosis in approximately 60–70 % of cases. However, the sensitivity of urine cultures is lower in isolated genital TB.2 Modern molecular diagnostic tools, such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR) for Mycobacterium tuberculosis, have enhanced the diagnostic yield, with sensitivity rates approaching 90 % and specificity rates over 95 %.1The standard treatment for testicular tuberculosis follows WHO guidelines with a four-drug regimen: isoniazid (300 mg/day), rifampin (600 mg/day), pyrazinamide (1500–2000 mg/day), and ethambutol (1200 mg/day) for two months. This is followed by isoniazid and rifampin for an additional four months.5 Treatment completion rates exceed 90 %, and cure rates are above 95 % with adherence.1Close monitoring is crucial as up to 20 % of patients on anti-tuberculosis therapy experience side effects. Hepatotoxicity from isoniazid and rifampin occurs in 2–8%, requiring liver function tests.2 Ethambutol can cause optic neuropathy in 1–3%, necessitating vision screening. Pyrazinamide is linked to hyperuricemia and gouty arthritis in 5–10 % of patients.1Fertility concerns are significant in testicular tuberculosis. Azoospermia, oligospermia, and impaired sperm motility affect 30–50 % of advanced cases.1 Unilateral involvement may preserve hormonal function, but bilateral disease, affecting 5–10 % of cases, often results in hypogonadism and infertility.2 Semen analysis before and after treatment is essential, with long-term follow-up recommended for those with preserved spermatogenesis.44

Conclusion

Testicular tuberculosis is a rare but important differential diagnosis for testicular masses, particularly in endemic regions. Prompt recognition, imaging, and histopathological confirmation are essential for guiding treatment and preventing complications.

Consent

The patient provided informed consent after receiving detailed information regarding the study and its implications.

Funding resources

This research and the publication expenses were solely supported by the author, and no specific grant was received from any public, commercial, or non-profit sectors.

CRediT authorship contribution statement

Salim Lachkar: Conceptualization, Data curation, Investigation, Methodology, Writing – original draft. Mamoun Diouri: Data curation, Investigation. Ahmed Ibrahimi: Investigation, Methodology, Supervision. Imad Boualaoui: Methodology, Supervision, Writing – original draft. Hachem El Sayegh: Supervision, Validation, Writing – original draft. Yassine Nouini: Conceptualization, Supervision, Validation.

Conflict of interest

None.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 41-year-old male
- **Chief Complaint:** Chronic left scrotal swelling, fistulization, and purulent discharge.
- **History of Present Illness:** Left scrotal swelling persisted for over eight weeks, progressively increasing to a diameter of 15 cm, fistulizing three weeks prior with associated purulent discharge. No systemic symptoms such as fever, night sweats, weight loss, or pulmonary tuberculosis indicators. No urinary symptoms.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** No history of scrotal trauma or tuberculosis exposure.
- **Family History:** None reported.
- **Physical Examination:** Enlarged and tense left hemiscrotum with a fistulous opening discharging purulent fluid. Left testis firm, irregular, and non-tender; right testis normal. No lymphadenopathy or significant findings in chest/abdominal exams.
- **Ancillary Tests:**
  - **Scrotal Ultrasound:** Enlarged left testis with heterogeneous echotexture, necrotic areas, microcalcifications, and scrotal wall thickening. Doppler showed no vascular flow, suggesting non-malignant pathology.
  - **Chest X-ray:** Normal.
  - **Tumor Markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH):** Normal.
  - **QuantiFERON-TB Gold Test:** Positive.
  - **CT scan (abdomen/pelvis):** No additional genitourinary or systemic lesions identified.
  - **Histopathology:** Caseating granulomas and acid-fast bacilli on Ziehl-Neelsen staining, consistent with tuberculosis.
  - **Complete Blood Count (CBC):** Mild anemia; elevated ESR (42 mm/h).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Step 1: Consideration of malignancy:** Initial imaging raised suspicion for malignancy due to the heterogeneity and necrosis of the testicular tissue. However, normal tumor markers and the lack of vascular flow on Doppler suggested an infectious rather than neoplastic process. Histopathology excluded malignancy.
- **Step 2: Suspected bacterial orchitis/abscess:** The chronic course, lack of systemic symptoms, and negative urinary findings made bacterial orchitis or abscess less likely.
- **Step 3: Tuberculosis (TB) suspicion:** The positive QuantiFERON-TB Gold test and endemicity of tuberculosis made genitourinary tuberculosis a plausible diagnosis. The imaging features (necrosis, calcifications, and scrotal thickening) and histopathological findings of caseating granulomas further confirmed TB.
- **Exclusion of other conditions:** Conditions like sarcoidosis and idiopathic granulomatous orchitis were excluded due to the presence of caseation and acid-fast bacilli.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The final diagnosis is **isolated testicular tuberculosis.** This conclusion was based on chronic scrotal swelling, positive tuberculosis-specific testing, and definitive histopathological findings of caseating granulomas with acid-fast bacilli. The absence of systemic or pulmonary TB features supported the isolated nature of the disease.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let's tackle this case. The patient is a 41-year-old male with chronic left scrotal swelling that's been there for over eight weeks. It got bigger, up to 15 cm, and then developed a fistula with pus discharge. No systemic symptoms like fever or weight loss. No urinary issues either. 

First, I need to think about the possible causes of scrotal swelling. Common things like epididymitis, orchitis, hydrocele, or a tumor come to mind. But since it's chronic and there's a fistula, maybe something more specific. The ultrasound showed an enlarged testis with necrotic areas and microcalcifications. The Doppler showed no blood flow, which is interesting. Usually, tumors might have increased vascularity, but here it's absent, pointing away from malignancy. Tumor markers are normal too, so that supports a non-cancerous cause.

Then there's the QuantiFERON-TB test, which is positive. That's a strong indicator of tuberculosis exposure. But the patient has no history of TB exposure or symptoms like night sweats. However, extrapulmonary TB can present in the genitourinary system without pulmonary involvement. The histopathology showing caseating granulomas and acid-fast bacilli (AFB) on staining is a key point. Caseating granulomas are classic for TB. The presence of AFB confirms mycobacterial infection.

Other possibilities: Could it be a bacterial abscess? But the histopathology doesn't support that. Fungal infections might cause granulomas, but AFB staining is positive here. Sarcoidosis can cause granulomas too, but they're non-caseating. The CT scan didn't find other lesions, which might have suggested sarcoid. Also, the positive TB test tilts it towards TB.

The chronicity, the positive TB test, and the histopathology findings all line up. Even though the chest X-ray is normal, TB can affect the genitourinary system without lung involvement. The elevated ESR and mild anemia are consistent with an ongoing chronic infection like TB. The fistula formation is also a known complication of TB when it involves the scrotum or epididymis, leading to breakdown of tissue and sinus tracts.

So putting it all together: the patient has genital tuberculosis affecting the left testis, leading to necrosis, granuloma formation, and fistulization. The absence of other systemic symptoms doesn't rule it out since extrapulmonary TB can be localized. The positive Quantiferon, histology, and imaging all support TB as the diagnosis.
results.content:
```
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient presents with chronic left scrotal swelling, fistulization, and purulent discharge. The absence of systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss) or urinary symptoms narrows the differential to localized infectious, inflammatory, or granulomatous processes.  
<step 2> Scrotal ultrasound findings (heterogeneous testicular echotexture, necrosis, microcalcifications, absent vascular flow) and normal tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) argue against malignancy. Non-malignant etiologies like chronic infection or granulomatous disease are prioritized.  
<step 3> The positive QuantiFERON-TB Gold test indicates latent or active tuberculosis (TB) exposure. While chest X-ray and CT are normal, extrapulmonary TB—particularly genitourinary TB—can occur without pulmonary involvement.  
<step 4> Histopathology confirming caseating granulomas with acid-fast bacilli (AFB) on Ziehl-Neelsen staining is pathognomonic for tuberculosis. This finding, combined with the clinical and laboratory evidence, definitively establishes TB as the cause of the scrotal pathology.  
<step 5> Elevated ESR and mild anemia further support a chronic inflammatory/infectious process. The absence of other lesions on imaging and normal CBC (aside from anemia) exclude disseminated infections or malignancies.  

### Answer:
Tuberculous epididymo-orchitis with scrotal fistulization.

reasoning_eval.step: The patient presents with chronic left scrotal swelling, fistulization, and purulent discharge. The absence of systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss) or urinary symptoms narrows the differential to localized infectious, inflammatory, or granulomatous processes.

Scrotal ultrasound findings (heterogeneous testicular echotexture, necrosis, microcalcifications, absent vascular flow) and normal tumor markers (AFP, beta-hCG, LDH) argue against malignancy. Non-malignant etiologies like chronic infection or granulomatous disease are prioritized.

The positive QuantiFERON-TB Gold test indicates latent or active tuberculosis (TB) exposure. While chest X-ray and CT are normal, extrapulmonary TB—particularly genitourinary TB—can occur without pulmonary involvement.

Histopathology confirming caseating granulomas with acid-fast bacilli (AFB) on Ziehl-Neelsen staining is pathognomonic for tuberculosis. This finding, combined with the clinical and laboratory evidence, definitively establishes TB as the cause of the scrotal pathology.

Elevated ESR and mild anemia further support a chronic inflammatory/infectious process. The absence of other lesions on imaging and normal CBC (aside from anemia) exclude disseminated infections or malignancies.

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial imaging raised suspicion for malignancy due to the heterogeneity and necrosis of the testicular tissue, but normal tumor markers and lack of vascular flow on Doppler suggested an infectious rather than neoplastic process; histopathology excluded malignancy.
Chronic course, lack of systemic symptoms, and negative urinary findings made bacterial orchitis or abscess less likely.

Positive QuantiFERON-TB Gold test and endemicity of tuberculosis made genitourinary tuberculosis a plausible diagnosis; imaging features (necrosis, calcifications, and scrotal thickening) and histopathological findings of caseating granulomas further confirmed TB.
Conditions like sarcoidosis and idiopathic granulomatous orchitis were excluded due to the presence of caseation and acid-fast bacilli.

Final diagnosis is isolated testicular tuberculosis, based on chronic scrotal swelling, positive tuberculosis-specific testing, and definitive histopathological findings of caseating granulomas with acid-fast bacilli; absence of systemic or pulmonary TB features supported the isolated nature of the disease.
